sourceMovie.title: Frankenstein
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Frankenstein (2025) stuck because it commits to its drama premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Guillermo del Toro's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The drama and fantasy blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Mary Shelley's Frankenstein
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Frankenstein clicked for you, Mary Shelley's Frankenstein (1994) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama and horror, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Byzantium
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Frankenstein clicked for you, Byzantium (2013) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama and fantasy, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Frankenstein?
faq.answer: Try Mary Shelley's Frankenstein, Byzantium, Lords of Chaos—each shares DNA with Frankenstein while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Frankenstein?
faq.answer: Frankenstein (2025) is best described as Drama, Fantasy, Horror. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Frankenstein worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.7/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this drama lane.
